title: AP: DHS Given $20M for Body Cameras But Houston ICE Agents Had None
excerpt: An Associated Press investigation revealed that despite Congress allocating $20 million for ICE body cameras in April 2026 and former Secretary Noem's February promise to rapidly deploy them, none of the agents involved in the fatal Houston shooting of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo were equipped with cameras.
content:
An Associated Press investigation published July 10, 2026 documented a significant accountability gap: Congress had allocated $20 million specifically for "the procurement, deployment, and operations of body-worn cameras" for ICE and CBP officers conducting immigration enforcement tasks, yet the agents who fatally shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston were wearing no cameras.

DHS had promised in February 2026, following the fatal shootings of U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, that it would "rapidly acquire and deploy" body cameras nationwide. Nearly six months later, that promise had not been fulfilled for the Houston field office. DHS blamed government funding lapses driven by congressional Democrats, a claim Democrats disputed as "ludicrous."

  • Congress allocated the $20 million body camera funding in April 2026 via the reconciliation process.
  • Rep. Sylvia Garcia stated: "Even after we've given ICE specifically $20 million for body cameras and Kristi Noem promised in February of this year that she was going to purchase them and get them in the field, here we were in Houston and the agents didn't have them."
  • DHS said body cameras "have been deployed to more than half the field offices," with the remainder to receive them within 60 days.
  • The absence of body camera footage has forced investigators to rely solely on witness accounts, physical evidence, and available bystander video — a pattern that has recurred in multiple prior disputed ICE shooting incidents.
  • A court filing from January 2026 in Minnesota previously established that body cameras had not been deployed to ICE deportation officers in the St. Paul field office at that time.
source: Associated Press / U.S. News & World Report
mediaPlaceholder: Congressional budget document showing $20M body camera allocation; side-by-side timeline of DHS deployment promises versus incidents without cameras
